item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My favourite entry of Series V so far

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really liked this one, have your 100th upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah. Thanks for clarifying. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not quite! Here's how I intended it:

Some spoilers
Some spoilers

1. Panners There is no gold in Kosser valley. When those first two panners showed up, theirs was the greatest desire. They wanted to find gold in the river.

2. Feldspar Then, the town was built. Sure, everyone wanted gold, so there was gold. There was hardly one single strongest desire, though. People wanted many things: a better home, a better life, love, wealth, etc. There was only so much "power" in Feldspar.

3. Lovers Julia and Raquel fell in love. They wanted to be together so badly. Together, their desire became the strongest. But when they were pressured to marry, and stop seeing each other, that desire changed. Walsh's last memory of Julia shows this the most clearly: this town needs gold to survive. They resented that the town would trade gold for their happiness.

Think of the twins like a manifestation of all this. Julia and Raquels desire to have a family together, to protect their own families, and the pressures of giving the town the gold it desired, coupled with their resentment. They saw that lust for gold like a cruel sickness so: tumors.

4. Feldspar again As the sickeness grew more potent, combined with delusion and anger, the desire of the town grew stronger than Julia and Raquel's. They wanted them dead. They wanted the babies gone. They wanted all of this to be over.

5. Hallewell Then, Douglass Halewell arrives. He discovers the tumors. Ruthless and brutal as he is, his desire becomes the strongest. He wants these people to keep on producing gold, his literal cash cows.

6. Foundation When the Foundation takes control… well, coupled with 05's last statement, that much should be clear. The Foundation isn't about neutralizing anomalies; it's about containing them, continually suppressing them, and gaining what benefits they can from it. That's exactly what happens in Feldspar. The "rock formations" didn't exist until the Foundation showed up. We wanted a predictable, rational reason for this to happen. We wanted an explainable anomaly, not cursed gold babies. Hence, the rocks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just so I'm getting this right…

+
-

The anomaly makes desires come true, which results in everyone growing precious metal growths, and the blame on the children turned them into super dense metal clusters? Also, why weren't the two families affected? Because they didn't want gold?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the read :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The ending of this was a very satisfying conclusion to the story in all the wrong and right ways. Very good SCP overall.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks so much!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i really enjoyed this SCP :) great work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very good catch. I'll correct that now!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One issue I see:

This continued until 2004, when Mrs. Dana Walsh, a Feldspar resident, placed a call with the U.S. National Guard.

The interview is dated 03/08/1998

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks so much for the read Hippo! Ellipses are ranked just under "s vs 's" in my hurdles as a writer. I'm working to improve within this format.

You're also right that this article is essentially a short-story sandwich, with the article as the bread. I experimented a bit trying to break up Mrs. Walsh's dialogue, intersecting it with the researcher's observations or scoring it with article elements, but it all felt like padding more than pacing. I didn't want to waste the readers time. In the end, I settled on making it just an old woman telling one story, and the Foundation telling another.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like there are parts of the interview that are formatted "oddly" (ellipsis before a phrase or as a method to designate pauses always bugs me), and it came precariously close at several points to reading more like a novel than an article.

That being said, I was genuinely surprised by this. The dialogue reads like a story, but it's quite a good one; the writing is clean and compelling, and the ending is deeply satisfying. A very, very easy upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the read Jimmy my boy!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also corrected. You guys are on it!

item.content:encoded: Really well done. I love the way it's written and it has great attention to detail. Follows protocols well and makes sense when read. Awesome job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man this is gold

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks so much aismallard! I'll get to those edits tonight. Appreciated as always.

EDIT: Edits made. One day I will conquer my one true enemy: 's vs s.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, so you've uncovered my secret.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice storytelling, was an enjoyable read.

As is tradition:

+ Show nitpicks
- Hide nitpicks

U.S. Legal system

legal

Naturally occurring radiation or hazardous chemicals in the area.

Personally I would remove "in the area" since it's a footnote, but it's a preference thing.

She's got my husbands eyes

husband's

She's only 5 years old.

Since this is a transcript of spoken word, I would use "five".

Maddie had her uncles old radio.

uncle's

We were all just a bunch of miners daughters

miner's

with the butchers son

butcher's

Mr. Hallewell brought in some city-boy tycoons son

tycoon's

I saw it's eyes.

its

Simultaneous exposure was required to form SCP-4938-1 tumorous growths.

Should be "is required" to match the tense.

The Department of Finance has been tasked with regulating the export of Gold

gold

I also like these stories showing areas where the Foundation exploits the anomalous to make money and fund their operations. All those resources have to come from somewhere…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well done, as always. You're on fire.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for catching both of those. I'll make the first edit, and you're right, that "fallout" line is some spill-over from a previous draft. It doesn't suit the tone anymore.

EDIT: Changed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Extremely minor thing: "Simultaneous exposure was required to form -1 tumorous growths.": I'd suggest using the full "SCP-4938-1" notation here because it's a little ambiguous and I initially interpreted it as being "minus one tumorous growths".

The "no investigation? No fallout? *silence* Er, pardon my phrasing" section feels weird to me? I think you're trying to be all "fallout comes across as insensitive because of the radiation" but fallout specifically comes from nuclear explosions, and it feels a little weird to me that she's sensitive about the word?

item.title: SCP-4938: Sweat from the Sun
item.content:encoded:
The spirit thrives down deep inside
That's where it's home
So let's break the ties where evil lies
And get in the zone

Gold (gold) dust is falling
It's raining down
Gold (gold) dust is falling
It's raining down

Gold Spoilers
Gold Spoilers

There's only one anomaly. The strongest desires become reality in the town of Feldspar.

Silver Spoilers
Silver Spoilers

O5-4 claims Edwin Hallewell died in 1921. The initial picture was taken in 1922. The Foundation is lying.

I was inspired to write this when researching neutron stars. It is currently theorized that heavy elements such as platinum, uranium and gold are created when two neutron stars collide. This celestial collision will cause an explosion, tearing them apart, and scattering their metals throughout the universe.

Kosser Valley, Feldspar, and all other locations in this article are fictional. Except Colorado, I think. Image sources are here, here, and here. All under the Creative Commons Attribution-Share Alike 3.0 Unported license.

Thanks to:
BlueJones
Rounderhouse
ManyMeats
For their suggestions and crits. Also thanks to the editors adding forgotten tags and fixing mistakes.

► More T Rutherford Articles and Tales ◄
